title: Distributed Locking & Coordination
description: Distributed locking coordinates access to shared resources across process boundaries. This file covers lock types (lease-based, semaphore, mutex), ZooKeeper locks (ephemeral sequential znodes), Redis Redlock, Google Chubby, fencing tokens, lease expiration handling, and the common failure modes (split-brain, thundering herd, lock starvation). SAP engineers familiar with enqueue locking will find direct analogies to distributed lock managers.

description.value:
Distributed locks are fundamentally different from single-process locks. In a single process, locks are bounded by thread/process lifecycle. In distributed systems, locks are bounded by leases — time-bound permissions that expire if the holder doesn't renew. Leases solve the 'what happens when the lock holder crashes?' problem.

Lease-based lock mechanics: (1) Client requests a lock from the lock service. (2) Lock service grants the lock with a TTL (lease duration, typically 10-60 seconds). (3) Client must periodically renew the lease (heartbeat/refresh) before the TTL expires. (4) If the client crashes, the lease expires and another client can acquire the lock. (5) If the client's work outlasts the lease, it must renew or abort the work.

Critical problem — lease expiration + work completion race: Client A acquires a lock with a 10-second lease. Client A starts work. At t=8s, GC pauses Client A for 5 seconds. At t=10s, the lease expires. Client B acquires the lock. At t=13s, Client A's GC ends. Client A completes its work and releases the lock — which was already released (lease expired) and re-acquired by Client B. Result: both clients believe they hold the lock simultaneously — a split-brain condition.

Solution — fencing tokens: When Client A acquires a lock, the lock service gives it a fencing token (monotonically increasing number). Client A includes the fencing token in every write to the protected resource. The resource rejects writes with stale fencing tokens. Example: lock service gives token 5 to Client A. Client B acquires the lock later and gets token 6. Client A's writes with token 5 are rejected because the resource has seen token 6. This is the approach used by Google Chubby and Apache ZooKeeper.

SAP Bridge: SAP enqueue locking is centralized (single lock server). The lock server has a lease mechanism — if a work process crashes, the lock entry is released after a timeout. But SAP does NOT use fencing tokens — if two processes believe they hold the same lock, both can write to the same data. This is safe in SAP because the database transaction isolation prevents conflicting writes. In distributed systems, fencing tokens are essential because there is no central transaction coordinator.

adversarialResponse.challenge: "We can't use Redis Redlock because it's not a consensus-based lock. The 'Redlock debate' between antirez and Martin Kleppmann showed that Redlock is not safe for distributed locking. Why would we use it at FAANG?"
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "Redlock is proven and used at many large companies. It's safe enough for most use cases." (vague, dismissive)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "You're referring to Martin Kleppmann's critique of Redlock in 'How to do distributed locking' (2016). The core issue: Redlock is not a consensus system — it uses majority writes without a global monotonically increasing clock. This means two clients can believe they hold the same lock simultaneously if clock drift causes unequal lease expiry. antirez's response was that Redlock is designed for 'best-effort' mutual exclusion where occasional overlap is acceptable. At FAANG, we use Redlock in two contexts: (1) Non-critical locks — rate limiting counters, cache warming coordination, job deduplication — where occasional double-execution is harmless. (2) Critical locks with fencing — we pair Redlock with a fencing token generated by a monotonically increasing counter (etcd or PostgreSQL sequence). The resource rejects writes with stale tokens. For critical locks (financial transactions, resource allocation), we use etcd locks with Raft consensus — not Redlock. The right tool depends on whether 'exactly one' is a correctness requirement or a performance optimization."
adversarialResponse.pattern: In the 'Redlock debate,' the Staff+ answer is: neither side is wrong — they disagree on the correctness model. The Staff+ engineer understands both positions and can articulate when each is acceptable. Never take a dogmatic stance on the Redlock debate; show that you understand the clock drift assumption and have mitigation strategies.

description.value:
ZooKeeper provides distributed locking through its znode (ZooKeeper data node) abstraction. The most powerful pattern is the ephemeral sequential znode — a node that is automatically deleted when the client's session ends and has a monotonically increasing sequence number appended to its name.

Read-Write Lock using ZooKeeper: (1) The lock is represented by a parent znode (e.g., /locks/resource_x). (2) Each client that wants the read lock creates an ephemeral sequential child znode prefixed with 'read-'. Each client that wants the write lock creates an ephemeral sequential child znode prefixed with 'write-'. (3) A read lock is granted to a client if: the client's znode is the lowest-numbered read-znode, OR all lower-numbered znodes are read- znodes (write- znodes block reads). (4) A write lock is granted when the client's znode is the lowest-numbered write-znode among all znodes. (5) Each client sets a watcher on the preceding znode in sequence to be notified when it's their turn.

Key property — lock ordering without polling: ZooKeeper's watcher mechanism means clients don't poll for lock availability. They set a one-shot watch on the preceding znode. When that znode is deleted (session timeout), the watcher fires, and the client checks if it's now the lock holder. This is a 'herd effect' avoidance pattern — only one client is woken up per lock release.

Limitations: (1) Read-write locks in ZooKeeper are 'fair' but not 'read-optimized' — if a write lock is waiting, new read locks must queue behind it (no read-side concurrency with pending writes). (2) ZooKeeper's write throughput is limited (~10K writes/second for a 3-node ensemble). For high-throughput locking, Redis or client-side sharding is needed. (3) ZooKeeper sessions are long-lived (default 30s timeout) — lock recovery after a crash takes at least one session timeout.

sections.title: Google Chubby — Lock Service for Coarse-Grained Synchronization

description.value:
Google Chubby is the lock service used by Google's infrastructure (GFS, Bigtable, MapReduce) for coarse-grained synchronization. It is the predecessor of ZooKeeper (ZooKeeper was inspired by Chubby's design). Understanding Chubby's design is a strong signal of distributed systems depth in Staff+ interviews.

Chubby design: (1) Five-node Paxos-based replicated log for consistency. (2) Clients hold locks using sessions with leases (typically 30-60 seconds). (3) Clients must periodically renew their session lease. If a client crashes, the session expires after the lease timeout. (4) Chubby uses a 'lock-delay' mechanism: when a session expires, the lock is not immediately released — it is delayed for a configurable period (30-60 seconds). This prevents the 'lock released due to transient network partition' problem. (5) Chubby's lock-delay mitigates the split-brain lock expiration problem without requiring fencing tokens (though fencing tokens are available).

Chubby's key insight — coarse-grained is better: Chubby was designed for locks that are held for seconds to hours (namespace master election, GFS chunk server assignment), not milliseconds (row-level locking). This is intentional: coarse-grained locks reduce lock service load (from 10M/s to 100/s). The lesson: if you need fine-grained distributed locks, you're probably doing something wrong with your architecture.

details: FAANG Interview Tip: Distributed locking is a common trap in system design interviews. The best candidate response is: 'Let's evaluate whether we actually need distributed locking.' Many systems can avoid distributed locks by using: (1) Optimistic concurrency control (compare-and-swap). (2) Partitioning — each partition has a single writer; no lock needed. (3) Idempotent operations — if we can tolerate duplicate execution, we don't need locks. Only reach for distributed locks when all three options are exhausted. This shows seniority.
